Okay, raise your hand if you still watch Disney movies. Anybody? Nobody? Okay, I'm talking to myself. This is the section where you get to take your prospect on the proverbial magic carpet ride. So you're going to be giving them a bird's eye view of the future that awaits them, you know, this whole new world, but you're going to be doing it through your product. So your product is that escape vehicle, the magic carpet that enables them to view this possibility, this new world of them being in. And you're going to do it with as much specificity and clarity as possible so that they don't need to think. You just kind of want them to chill and take it all in kind of like this guy. So I don't think David Ogilvy said it, but he definitely thought it. Your prospect is not a moron. She's your cat dressed up as Jasmine on a Roomba. So make it easy on her. Moving on. All cats aside. Other than your headline, the product tour and guarantee are two of the most carefully read parts of your sales page. So I've run this test in person probably close to a hundred times by now. So every time I ask someone to skim a sales page, they always land here every single time they land on the product tour and scroll down that until they get to the guarantee. So whereas your headline and lead get the most immediate attention, these sections will be absorbed by the skimmers, which means you've got to get it right and really make it do some heavy lifting. But the problem is it's also the section that most copywriters and business owners totally mail in. They just really want to be done at this point and the product tour feels like such a long, tedious task that they essentially list out a bunch of boring bullets, fired it off and hope for the best. So that's really not going to fly here. Our prospects definitely deserve better. Our products, you know, the ones that you or your client spent months or years developing, those deserve better. And this lesson will show you how to make that magic carpet fly. God, that was so lame. Okay, before I embarrass myself any further, here's what you're going to be coaching. So you're going to be coaching a future base. So you're future pacing the outcomes and your prospects role in producing them with your product. So once again, you're making your prospect the hero in this section, you know, even in the case of selling an online course, no one wants to learn. They want to see and prepave what they'll be doing with that information, how they are going to be using that information to generate a result. So even when the product is easy, something like popping a pill, they want to feel heroic in that process. The other thing you're going to be coaching here is excitement and intrigue around the process and the ownership experience. You know, you want to create an identity shift where your prospect experiences themselves as customers via active imagination and to just illustrate the power of that visualization of that active imagination. There's this myth, this legend, and I think it's been proven to be true now and confirmed, but it's of Major James Nesmith, who's been known as the prisoner of war golfer. So he was taken hostage, not hostage, as a POW in Vietnam. And for seven years every day in his cell, he was visualizing himself playing this 18 whole golf course back in his home in Georgia. And finally when he got released nearly a decade later and got back to that golf course in Georgia, he was able to shave off about 10 strokes off of his score by doing nothing but visualizing playing that every single day. So there's so much power in experiencing something in your mind first. And of course, there's so much literature on that topic, but the short note here is in this Aladdin product tour. You want to give your prospect every ability to really utilize our imagination of seeing themselves in that process. So each module or component, including the bonuses, needs to have its own mini sales page. Not only that, but each one needs to pass the, if this is all their getting test. The way this test goes is I like to go in with the mindset that if all your prospect was getting was this module or this lesson within it, would it be worth the price? And it's really that level of attention I want you to give each point. So if there's a valuable result or outcome that they can generate from that module or component that would make the whole product worth it, then mention it. So how are we going to do this? How do we give each component its own mini sales page while making sure that each would be worth it on its own? Well here is a little breakdown that I have. So each one would have its own three parts. So you do start with the module headline and the module headline could actually be the module title. And that will usually state the big outcome or promise that that specific component delivers. This will be the module lead. So this is going to be an elaboration of the promise and why that outcome matters. Mentioning how that outcome compounds on top of the outcomes of previous modules and components. So really creating this continuity between the different modules of your course or the different components of your product. And you're also going to mention the antithesis of that outcome. Here they'll struggle if they don't take action with what is included in that component. And finally you're going to have the module bullet. So these are going to be curiosity driven fascinations that really go deeper into the process, the how, and really ramp up the curiosity factor in a way that they absolutely need to see how they're going to achieve those results. So that is the breakdown on a very high level. And I want to remind you to engage them within it as much as possible. Give them the opportunity to live it and see themselves performing the actions that each component instructs or enables. So to enhance the ownership experience and identity ship, the tone and the language really should be a vivid play by play of what your prospect will be doing at each step. And that's how you really make sure your customer feels like the hero of the journey rather than the consumer of random spurts of information. So if you haven't already done so, if you've held off till now, it's really time to do that product walkthrough in section eight of the launch Bible. One frame I like to do for using this one that I use with clients is to pretend that you are delivering a live in person version of the product. So they invited you, you got invited to your prospects home or office on day one to deliver module one or feature one of your product. And you delivered it over a full day of workshops or whatever it takes to deliver it. And then you left at the end of that day. What are they excited about? What are they super energized about? What are they telling their spouse about at the end of the day? And why are they inviting you back to come on day two to continue the process? So bring it into real life and really try to get vivid about what that transformation looks like every step of the way with your product in a way that is exciting to them. You know, why do they feel based on one aspect of your product or module? Why do they feel like they've made the best decision of their life? You know, what will they no longer have to endure and what is now possible for them as a result and then really keep repeating that process for every module or component right through all the bonuses. So with all that said, take some time to write your Latin product tour. I know it's cute with cats dressed up as Jasmine on Roombas or Broombas, but it really does require you to dial down and get really clear about why each component or module of your product would be worth the price of admission. So with that said, give it a shot and meet me in the next video if you need more inspiration actually where I'm going to break down a few more examples where you could see this in action. I'll see you there.
